g.data-narration: Welcome to Operating System Generation. We explore how OS is configured for specific hardware.
SYSGEN is configuring an OS for specific hardware. It tailors the OS to the machine's components.
g.text: System Generation is the process of configuring and building an OS for a
specific hardware configuration. It sets parameters defining the hardware
environment and selects needed OS features.

g.data-narration: OS must adapt to different CPUs, memory sizes, storage devices, and I/O configurations.
The SYSGEN process transforms generic OS source code into a configured OS for specific machine.
SYSGEN gathers CPU details: type, cores, features like virtualization support, and clock speed.
g.text: • CPU Type: x86, x86-64, ARM, MIPS

g.data-narration: Memory information includes total RAM, memory configuration details, and available address space.
Device info covers types, addresses (I/O ports, IRQs, DMA channels), and device characteristics.
OS options define scheduling algorithms, max processes, file systems, network, and security settings.
Approach 1 recompiles the kernel with custom parameters. Most efficient but requires source code access.
g.text: Source code is modified with config parameters and recompiled to produce
a custom kernel binary. Used in Linux with make menuconfig.

g.data-narration: Approach 2 uses pre-compiled modules with configuration tables. Faster setup but larger distribution.
g.text: Pre-compiled modules for various hardware. OS reads config table at boot and

g.data-narration: Approach 3 uses loadable kernel modules. Most flexible - hardware changes need only module reload.
g.text: Small core kernel with many loadable modules. Hardware detected at boot and
runtime, modules dynamically loaded. Most flexible modern approach.

g.data-narration: Compare approaches: recompile offers best performance, table-driven is balanced, modular is most flexible.
Linux uses make menuconfig for kernel configuration. The .config file stores settings with y, m, n values.
g.text: Configuration tools: make config, make menuconfig (ncurses), make xconfig (Qt),
make gconfig (GTK), make defconfig, make oldconfig

g.data-narration: Linux build process: make menuconfig creates .config, then make compiles the kernel and modules.
Boot parameters pass kernel arguments via bootloader like GRUB. Examples: root=, mem=, quiet, splash.
SYSGEN configures OS for hardware. Three approaches: recompile (efficient), table-driven (balanced), modular (flexible).
g.text: • SYSGEN: Configuring OS for specific hardware
• Information needed: CPU, Memory, Devices, OS Options
• Three approaches: Recompile (best perf), Table-driven, Modular (most flexible)
Linux: make menuconfig → make → make modules → make install

g.data-narration: For exams: Know SYSGEN definition, four information types, three approaches, and Linux config process details.
g.text: 2. Four info types (CPU, Memory, Devices, OS Options)
4. Linux config: make menuconfig, .config (y/m/n), build process
5. LKM commands: insmod, rmmod, lsmod, modprobe
